Review Articles

Design of novel enzymes with the incorporation of noncanonical amino acids

Liang Chenga Institute of Bioengineering, College of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Zhejiang University, Hangzhou, Zhejiang, China
,
Lirong Yanga Institute of Bioengineering, College of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Zhejiang University, Hangzhou, Zhejiang, China
,
Zhongdi Songb Zhejiang Collaborative Innovation Center for Full-Process Monitoring and Green Governance of Emerging Contaminants, Interdisciplinary Research Academy, Zhejiang Shuren University, Hangzhou, Zhejiang, ChinaCorrespondencezhongdisong@foxmail.com
&
Haoran Yuc ZJU-Hangzhou Global Scientific and Technological Innovation Centre, Hangzhou, Zhejiang, ChinaCorrespondenceyuhaoran@zju.edu.cn
https://orcid.org/0000-0001-9012-4688
Pages 161-182 | Received 19 Nov 2024, Accepted 19 Nov 2025, Published online: 02 Feb 2026

Abstract

Enzymes are highly efficient biocatalysts known for their chemical, regio-, and stereoselectivity, making them valuable in industrial applications. While directed evolution has expanded the scope of enzyme-catalyzed reactions, the range of enzymatic reactions remains limited compared to reactions catalyzed by chemical catalysts. Computational enzyme design has achieved de novo enzyme design, but the approach is often complex, time-intensive, and has a low success rate. A promising strategy to design novel enzymes involves developing noncanonical amino acids (ncAAs) with catalytic potential and integrating them into protein scaffolds via genetic codon expansion technology. This method combines the novel reactivity of ncAAs with the high selectivity provided by protein scaffolds, significantly enhancing the diversity of enzyme-catalyzed reactions. This review discusses recent advancements in novel enzyme design using ncAAs, including those being used as catalytic groups, metal-coordinating groups, heme ligands, and photocatalytic groups. The article emphasizes the broad potential of using ncAAs in enzyme design to expand the diversity of enzyme-catalyzed reactions, and outlooks the potential applications of artificial intelligence technology in this area.

Keywords:

  • Enzyme design
  • noncanonical amino acids
  • genetic codon expansion
  • biocatalysis
  • chemical catalysis
  • artificial intelligence

Introduction

Enzymes are very powerful biocatalysts that have evolved over thousands of years to maintain the operation of various life systems. The high efficiency and chemo-, regio-, and stereoselectivity of enzymes make them attractive to be used in various industries [Citation1]. Moreover, most enzymes have been found to possess a characteristic of catalytic promiscuity, which allows the development of new-to-nature biocatalytic functions, effectively expanding the biocatalytic toolbox, particularly for organic synthesis [Citation2]. Protein engineering approaches facilitate the evolution of enzymes to accept a diverse range of substrates, beyond their natural specificities [Citation3]. However, in most of the protein engineering studies, the constructed enzyme mutants could only catalyze the substrates with the same reaction group as the natural substrate. Compared to chemical catalysts, the number of reactions catalyzed by enzymes are still few, despite the vast number of enzymes discovered in nature and developed through protein engineering [Citation4–6].

Many efforts have been made to engineer natural enzymes to catalyze new reactions. For instance, the cytochrome P450 has been engineered to catalyze various unnatural reactions such as inserting carbene into a broad spectrum of bonds, including C≡C, activated C-H, N-H, S-H, and even the more exotic B-H and Si-H bond [Citation7–9]. However, since the activity and specificity of enzymes stem from complex active site geometries, with functional residues organized and regulated for substrate recognition and transition state stabilization, this inherent complexity poses significant challenges in the design of enzymes for novel tasks [Citation10]. Up to now, the majority of enzyme design efforts rely on directed evolution to identify rare and highly active catalysts from large libraries of enzyme variants, which is time and labor-consuming.

Additionally, computational enzyme design relying on transition state stabilization theories has also been applied in combination with directed evolution for generating enzymes with novel functionalities [Citation11,Citation12]. To obtain an artificial enzyme specifically catalyzing a reaction that is not naturally catalyzed by any enzymes, the target reaction transition state is first defined. After that, a protein scaffold for stabilizing the reaction transition state is searched from a database and designed using a computation program such as RosettaDesign [Citation13–15]. Generally, the initial design obtained only shows weak activity toward the target reaction, which could be further improved by directed evolution [Citation16]. Following this protocol, various artificial enzymes have been created, including those acquiring the ability to catalyze the Kemp elimination, retro-aldol reaction, Diels-Alder reaction, and Morita − Baylis − Hillman reaction [Citation10,Citation17–26]. However, such processes require a profound understanding of catalytic mechanisms and accurate protein computational design, which is undoubtedly a formidable challenge.

The noncanonical amino acids (ncAAs) are the derivatives of natural amino acids that possess additional chemical groups such as ketone, aldehyde, azide, amide, nitro, sulfonate, and so forth. The genetic codon expansion (GCE) technology has the capability to incorporate ncAAs into proteins in a manner akin to programming [Citation27,Citation28]. Inspired by the way enzymes in nature expand their functionalities, such as recruiting cofactors or undergoing post-translational modifications, it is possible to introduce non-biological groups into proteins to directly exert new catalytic functions or recruit new cofactors to construct new functional enzymes using GCE technology [Citation29–31]. This would greatly narrow the gap between enzymatic catalysis and chemical catalysis. In theory, any existing reaction could be catalyzed by a “customized” enzyme [Citation5]. GCE technology, hence, shows a tremendous potential in enzyme design for novel reactivities [Citation32–34].

The increasing demand for high-performing biocatalysts requires not only novel enzymatic functionalities but also high catalytic activity and selectivity superior to current chemical catalysts [Citation4,Citation35]. This article mainly focuses on the advancements in the design of novel enzymes based on ncAAs, highlighting the expanded reaction scope enabled by these ncAAs and their crucial catalytic roles in novel enzymes (). Specifically, we review the studies regarding novel enzyme design using ncAAs, including those being catalytic groups, metal-coordinating groups, heme ligands, and photocatalytic groups.

Table 1. The noncanonical amino acids involved in the review, including their critical roles in enzymes and the reactions catalyzed by the designed artificial enzymes.

Display Table

ncAAs as catalytic residues

Natural enzymes are composed of 20 amino acids, but less than half of these amino acids have moieties that can act as acids, bases, or nucleophiles (electron-pair donors) in enzymatic catalysis cycles [Citation58,Citation59]. The incorporation of ncAAs with diverse functional side chains, particularly those harboring potential catalytic moieties, has the potential to unlock a vast number of novel enzymatic reactions [Citation60].

Primary-amine-containing ncAAs for iminium catalysis

Both primary amines and secondary amines can promote iminium catalysis for important reactions. Depending on the nucleophilic reagents and catalysts used, iminium ion intermediates can undergo various reaction pathways, such as 1,4-addition, cycloaddition, and 1,2-addition, thereby generating a wide range of synthetically useful chemical reactions [Citation61]. Natural enzymes that can catalyze such reactions are typically limited to those containing a proline residue at the chain terminus, such as 4-oxalocrotonate tautomerase, or various aldolases with lysine as the key catalytic residue [Citation62–65]. The N-terminal proline-containing secondary amine acts as a general base, facilitating nucleophilic catalysis, while the key lysine residue containing primary amine in aldolase forms a Schiff base with the substrate to activate the aldol donor, leading to the formation of a highly nucleophilic enamine species [Citation66–69]. These enzymes play a crucial role in C-C bond formation in biological systems and provide valuable insights into the development of artificial enzymes.

The ncAAs containing primary or secondary amine could hence be introduced into proteins, acting as catalytic residues of artificial enzymes for iminium catalysis (). By incorporating p-aminophenylalanine (pAF) into the hydrophobic pocket of the lactococcal multidrug resistance regulator (LmrR, PDB: 3F8F) protein scaffold, Roelfes et al. designed an artificial enzyme capable of catalyzing hydrazine formation, where the aniline side chain of pAF acted as an efficient nucleophilic catalyst () [Citation36]. The LmrR scaffold, a homodimer protein with a mixed hydrophobic pocket, has the ability to bind hydrophobic substrates, making it an attractive platform for enzyme design [Citation70]. The substrate can enter the hydrophobic cavity of the LmrR scaffold easily, thereby increasing its effective molar concentration to accelerate the reaction. V15, N19, M89, and F93, which lined the anticipated binding pocket, were selected as candidate sites. The best LmrR mutant, LmrR_V15pAF, with V15 substituted to pAF, exhibited a catalytic activity more than two orders of magnitude higher than simple anilines and approximately 10-fold higher than the tyrosine mutant LmrR_V15Y [Citation36]. A mutant LmrR_V15pAF/A11L/N19M/A92R/F93H (LmrR_V15pAF_LMRH) was then obtained by iterative site-saturation mutagenesis, which exhibited a nearly 50-fold increase in kcat compared to LmrR_V15pAF () [Citation71]. This represents a successful example of enzyme design for new reactivity with high activity through the introduction of ncAAs followed by directed evolution. Although pAF is not essential for this reaction, as the natural tyrosine variant (LmrR_V15Y) can still catalyze it, this approach paves the way for developing innovative biocatalysts with great potential, greatly advancing green synthesis.

Figure 1. pAF as a catalytic residue for iminium catalysis. a. The reaction catalyzed by the artificial enzyme LmrR_V15pAF (PDB: 6I8N, V15 mutated to pAF), referring to chromogenic hydrazone formation between 4-hydrazino-7-nitro-2,1,3-benzoxadiazole and 4-hydroxybenzaldehyde. b. The artificial enzyme LmrR_V15pAF catalyzed the Friedel-Crafts reaction of 2-methyl-indole with trans-2-hexenal. C. The artificial enzyme, LmrR_V15pAF_Cu(II)-phen, with two catalytic sites (a pAF residue for the activation of the enal and Cu(II)-phen for the generation and delivery of the enolate nucleophile), synergistically catalyzed the Michael addition reaction of α,β-unsaturated aldehydes with 1-(1-methyl-1H-imidazol-2-yl)-2-phenylethan-1-one. d. LmrR_V15pAF_Cu(II)-phen catalyzed the tandem Michael addition/enantioselective protonation reaction of p-methoxy-substituted ketone with acrolein in water. The tables summarize the catalytic parameters obtained for the initial non-optimized variant and the best variant after a mutagenesis study.

The LmrR_V15pAF artificial enzyme was also applied to conduct Friedel − Crafts alkylation reactions, where the pAF acted as a catalytic residue to react with the α, β-unsaturated aldehyde substrates to generate imine intermediates () [Citation37]. These intermediates subsequently reacted with a second substrate to facilitate the overall transformation. For instance, the artificial enzyme LmrR_V15pAF catalyzed the alkylation of 2-methyl-indole with trans-2-hexenal, yielding a conversion rate of 42% at 45% e.e. (), significantly higher than the reaction catalyzed by aniline itself. Through systematic alanine scanning and site-directed mutagenesis at key residues, the engineered variant LmrR_V15pAF/L18R/S95G/M89N (LmrR_V15pAF_RGN) exhibited 74% conversion and 87% enantioselectivity. This further illustrates how to incorporate small molecule catalysts to design ncAA-based enzymes showing high activity and enantioselectivity.

The artificial enzyme LmrR_V15pAF was further designed for synergistic catalysis [Citation38]. The aniline side chain of pAF acted as a nucleophilic catalytic group, forming an iminium ion intermediate with the α, β-unsaturated aldehyde to initiate activation. Synergistically, the Cu(II)-phenanthroline supramolecularly anchored to W96 of the scaffold protein LmrR could activate the Michael donor, 1-(1-methyl-1H-imidazol-2-yl)-2-phenylethan-1-one, to generate the nucleophilic enolate (). This resulted in a Michael addition to the activated crotonaldehyde to get an aldehyde product with two chiral centers at a yield of 54%. The reaction with crotonaldehyde gave excellent enantioselectivities for both diastereomers with 98% and 86% e.e., respectively [Citation38]. Compared to LmrR_V15pAF, the LmrR_V15pAF/M8L variant exhibited significant improvements in both stereoselectivity and reactivity, affording over 93% e.e., 6:1 d.r., and 82% yield. Such a demonstration underscores the potential of artificial enzymes in catalyzing intricate reactions and introduces novel pathways for designing efficient biocatalysts. This synergistic catalytic strategy of placing two catalytic groups at the center of the scaffold enzyme overcomes the entropy cost limitation caused by the proximity of two substrates during the catalytic process, allowing for the catalysis of complex reactions within the same enzyme molecule.

In addition, the artificial enzyme LmrR_V15pAF successfully catalyzed the tandem Michael addition/enantioselective protonation reaction in water, displaying an excellent enantioselectivity [Citation39]. In this reaction, the p-methoxy-substituted ketone was used as a Michael donor, and the activated acrolein derivative was the Michael receptor, obtaining a 28% yield, 10:1 d.r., and 99% e.e. In contrast, the variant LmrR_V15pAF/M8L exhibited both further improved stereoselectivity and reactivity with a 65% yield, >20:1 d.r., and >99% e.e. for the major diastereomer (). These studies revealed that various challenging reactions could be carried out by utilizing genetically encoded ncAAs to activate electrophiles and supramolecular-bound metal complexes to activate nucleophiles.

Secondary-amine-containing ncAAs for iminium catalysis

L-Proline and its analogs, whose secondary amine groups can form imine or enamine intermediates with carbonyl groups, are important asymmetric catalytic reagents used in chemical synthesis. They can catalyze a range of critical reactions, including Knoevenagel condensation, cycloadditions, aldol reactions, Henry condensation, Friedel–Crafts alkylation, and conjugated additions such as Michael addition [Citation72,Citation73]. Integrating imine-based ncAAs into protein scaffolds to design novel enzymes could significantly broaden the range of reactions catalyzed by enzymes.

Using GCE technology, Gran-Scheuch et al. introduced N6-D-Prolyl-L-Lysine (NDL) into the LmrR scaffold, obtaining an artificial enzyme LmrR_V15NDL, which was evaluated for catalyzing the Michael addition of nitromethane to cinnamaldehyde [Citation40]. However, no further enzyme engineering was performed, and the conversion rate was only 31% with a 23% e.e. ( (1)). Similarly, Thomas L. Williams and Irshad M. Taily achieved a cinnamaldehyde reduction reaction with the best mutant LmrR_F93NDL at a conversion rate of 58%, compared to the 3% of the wild-type LmrR scaffold ( (2)) [Citation41]. NDL formed an iminium ion with the α, β-unsaturated carbonyl substrate, and hydride transfer occurred from the pro-R position of NADPH to Cβ of the iminium intermediate.

Figure 2. NDL as a secondary-amine catalytic residue. a. Incorporation of NDL into protein scaffold LmrR and the artificial retro-aldolase RA95.5-8 (RA95.5-8) to design three artificial enzymes, LmrR_V15NDL, LmrR_F93NDL, and RA95.5-8_S81NDL. These novel enzymes respectively catalyzed (1) the Michael addition of nitromethane to cinnamaldehyde, (2) cinnamaldehyde reduction reaction, and (3) the cyclopropanation reaction of α, β-unsaturated aldehydes.

An artificial enzyme containing NDL using the RA95.5-8 TIM barrel as a scaffold protein was also constructed, which was successfully used to catalyze an asymmetric cyclopropanation reaction of α, β-unsaturated aldehydes [Citation42]. Based on the crystal structure of RA95.5-8, nine sites were selected as candidates. Among these, the mutant RA95.5-8_S81NDL demonstrated the highest protein expression level and afforded the best performance in terms of substrate conversion and selectivity. After four rounds of directed evolution, a variant (RA95.5-8_S81NDL/L231D/L159H/L210T/V208I) was obtained that catalyzed a range of substrates at high conversion with high diastereoselectivity (up to 96: 4:0 d.r.) and enantioselectivity (up to >99: 1 e.r.) ( (3)). Although the chiral secondary amine catalysts can also catalyze this reaction, the yield and diastereoselectivity were significantly lower than those achieved by the artificial enzyme [Citation74]. Moreover, neither the scaffold protein alone nor the ncAA NDL can catalyze this reaction, indicating that the artificial enzyme functions as a catalytic entity where both the scaffold protein and the non-natural amino acid are indispensable and exhibit synergistic effects.

ncAAs as catalytic nucleophile

Natural hydrolases typically employ a catalytic triad containing an activated nucleophile paired with hydrogen-bond donors to stabilize oxyanion intermediates, whereas with the application of ncAAs, a functional hydrolase was generated with a non-canonical organocatalytic mechanism [Citation43]. A computationally designed enzyme scaffold BH32 was used as a template incorporating ncAA, which was initially designed for catalyzing the Morita–Baylis–Hillman reaction, with histidine playing a pivotal role as the nucleophilic catalytic group throughout the catalytic cycle [Citation25,Citation43]. In BH32, the catalytic His23 formed a stable acyl-enzyme complex with the substrate, which was difficult to hydrolyze. The authors employed GCE technology to replace His23 with Nδ-methyl histidine (NMH), leading to the formation of an organocatalytic esterase, BH32_H23NMH (OE1). This modification decreased the stability of the acyl-enzyme complex formed between His23 and the substrate, which increased the rate of hydrolytic turnover (). Through directed evolution, the resulting mutant OE1.3 showed a hydrolytic activity almost comparable with that of the natural hydrolase CUT190 () [Citation43,Citation75]. The methyl group of NMH is a weak electron-donating group, which increases the electron density on the imidazole ring through the inductive effect [Citation76]. As a result, the electron-donating ability of the side chain is enhanced, making it potentially more nucleophilic or electron-rich in certain reactions.

Figure 3. ncAAs as a nucleophilic catalytic residue. a. Incorporation of NMH into the scaffold BH32. Reaction scheme for the hydrolysis of fluorescein 2-phenylacetate catalyzed by BH32_H23 and BH32_H23_NMH. Reprinted from Figure 1 of Ref. [Citation41] with permission. Copyright 2019 Springer Nature. b. The artificial enzyme containing NMH catalyzed the Morita–Baylis–Hillman reaction. The tables summarize the catalytic parameters obtained for the initial non-optimized variant and the best variant after a mutagenesis study. c. Utilization of δVin-H to construct artificial enzymes within protein scaffolds, enabling hydrolysis of phenoxymethanol esters under low pH and styrene cyclopropanation under aerobic conditions. Copyright 2024 Springer Nature.

Recently, Wang and his colleagues successfully incorporated vinyl groups onto the imidazole δ-nitrogen atom of the histidine side chain, resulting in a novel and genetically encodable histidine analog, Nδ-vinylhistidine (δVin-H) () [Citation45]. This modification generated a more electron-deficient imidazole side chain, shifting the pKa of the histidine’s Nε-hydrogen from 7.07 to 5.71. At pH 5.5, the δVin-H of the catalytic center exhibited a significantly lower protonation level compared to NMH and histidine, resulting in enhanced nucleophilicity and increased hydrolytic activity under these physiologically relevant acidic conditions. In hydrolysis experiments of phenoxymethanol ester, the activities of OE1.3-wt, OE1.3-NMH, and OE1.3-δVin-H were evaluated at pH 5.5. As anticipated, the δVin-H in OE1.3 catalytic center demonstrated the highest conversion rate and the fastest hydrolysis reaction. Under aerobic conditions with 0.1% mol enzyme, the yield of Mb*-δVin-H for styrene cyclopropanation reached an impressive 80%, while Mb*-WT achieved only 30% under the same conditions ().

Hutton et al. also redesigned the BH32 protein by replacing the catalytic histidine with NMH to improve catalysis of the Morita–Baylis–Hillman reaction, resulting in a substantially altered evolutionary outcome. Although when the original nucleophile His23 was replaced with NMH in BH32, the activity was reduced. After successive rounds of laboratory evolution, the BH32.8_NMH1.8 variant containing 23 mutations was obtained, which was 2000-fold more active than the original BH32 () [Citation10,Citation44]. Structure and catalytic mechanism analysis revealed that the non-canonical nucleophile is beneficial but not essential to catalytic function, while the NMH was crucial in revealing the new mechanistic pathway observed in this enzyme in which a catalytic Arg124 was abandoned [Citation25,Citation44,Citation77].

ncAAs used for acid catalysis

Boric acids represent an exceptionally robust catalyst due to their distinct substrate activation models, such as the establishment of hydrogen bonds, the synthesis of acid anhydrides, Lewis or Brønsted acids behavior, and their preferential binding affinity toward alcohols [Citation78–80]. Roelfes et al. innovatively integrated boric acid into the structure of para-boronophenylalanine (pBoF), which was subsequently introduced into the LmrR protein scaffold at six positions (8, 11, 15, 89, 92, and 93). These residues resided on helices α1 and α4, lining the hydrophobic cavity along the protein dimer interface. The pBoF at different sites contributed to the formation of a distinct chemical microenvironment, yielding the best mutant boron enzyme LmrR_M89pBoF (PDB: 8QDF) () [Citation46]. The artificial enzyme initially catalyzed the condensation reaction between benzoin and hydroxylamine to form oxime with a moderate enantioselectivity of E value of 13. After several rounds of directed evolution, the obtained LmrR_M89pBoF/F93E/M8H/A92L variant eventually achieved an E value up to 146 with a significant improvement in conversion rate. This mutant provided higher rate accelerations than a large excess (500-fold concentration) of simple borate. Similarly, Xiang et al. utilized the artificial enzyme LmrR_M8pBoF to catalyze the Friedel–Crafts alkylation of α, β-unsaturated ketones and 2-methyl indole. Through directed evolution, the variant LmrR_M8pBoF/N88A/A92G/I4L demonstrated over 99% yield and enantioselectivity (94:6 e.r.) in model reactions () [Citation47]. The small-molecule boronic acid catalyst has shown its versatility in diverse bond-forming reactions, and incorporation of boronic acid into protein through ncAA represents a novel approach that broadens the scope of biocatalytic reactions while endowing boron catalysis with the capability to produce chiral products. This highlights that the strategy of anchoring organic catalytic small-molecule moieties into ncAA structures for enzyme design can effectively combine the selectivity of protein scaffolds with the catalytic properties of small-molecule groups, making it a practical and promising approach with broad applications.

Figure 4. pBoF as a catalytic residue for acid catalysis. Incorporation of pBoF into the scaffold LmrR. a. The designed enzymes provided a unique biocatalytic solution to the electrophilic activation of α-hydroxyketones, which selectively catalyzed the condensation reaction between benzoin and hydroxylamine to form oxime. Reprinted from Figure 1 of Ref. [Citation78] with permission. Copyright 2024 Springer Nature. b. the artificial enzyme LmrR_M8pBoF catalyzed Friedel–Crafts alkylation of α, β-unsaturated ketones and 2-methyl indole. The tables summarize the catalytic parameters obtained for the initial non-optimized variant and the best variant after a mutagenesis study.

ncAAs as metal-coordinating residues

Metalloenzymes, accounting for 70% of natural enzymes, demonstrate remarkable catalytic activity in water under mild conditions, facilitating diverse biological reactions within metabolism and the respiratory chain [Citation81]. Furthermore, they are greatly useful in industrial processes, such as the production of acrylamide through nitrile hydratase [Citation82]. The types of metal ions in metalloenzymes are relatively limited compared to metal catalysts, mainly consisting of metals that are commonly found in living organisms and are essential for life processes [Citation83]. To combine the stereo-, regio-selectivity and adaptability of enzymes with the reactivity of metal catalysts, a compelling strategy involves the transformation of existing proteins into functional metalloenzymes by incorporating catalytically active metal (complexes) into protein frameworks [Citation84,Citation85]. Chemical biologists have developed various strategies for constructing artificial metalloenzymes, including: (1) Conjugate anchoring, where non-native metal ions or metal complexes coordinate with amino acid residues on the protein surface; (2) Cofactor substitution, where natural cofactors like iron porphyrin complexes in hemoglobin are replaced with rigid planar metal complexes; (3) Covalent anchoring, where metal complexes with functional groups, such as maleimides, form covalent bonds with cysteine residues during (semi-)post-functionalization of proteins; and (4) Supramolecular assembly anchoring, involving interactions between metal cations and aromatic groups in proteins [Citation86].

Currently, available host-guest pairs for protein scaffolds in supramolecular assembly are limited, including biotin-streptavidin, coumarin-albumin, and heme-cytochrome C, which restricts the expansion of protein scaffolds [Citation87,Citation88]. Covalent strategies like maleimide-cysteine often require protein mutants with a single cysteine for selective modification. Both approaches need specific binding sites for anchoring metal cofactors and large, flexible linkers [Citation89]. Screening for artificial metalloenzymes with desired catalytic functions demands custom synthesis of linkers of various lengths and metal cofactor sizes, along with precise adjustments through amino acid mutations, resulting in high workload and lengthy optimization cycles [Citation90]. GCE technology enables the precise and direct incorporation of ncAAs with ligands into specific sites on the protein scaffold, eliminating the need for auxiliary flexible linker design and synthesis (). This approach avoids increased conformational freedom of the catalytic center and reduces the complexity of precise design associated with long linkers. Furthermore, directed evolution can be employed to fine-tune the spatial and electronic interactions of the second coordination sphere near the active sites of artificial metalloenzymes, allowing for targeted control of selectivity [Citation91].

Figure 5. BpyAla as a metal-coordinating residue. a. Schematic representation of genetic incorporation of an ncAA containing a metal-binding moiety as an additional ligand in a metalloenzyme. b. Incorporation of BpyAla into LmrR to chelate copper ions. These novel metalloenzyme-catalyzed Friedel-Crafts reaction of 2-methylindole with 1-(1-methyl-1H-imidazol-2-yl)but-2-en-1-one, resulting in opposite enantiomer. c. the artificial metalloenzyme catalyzed the ketenes hydration reaction of α, β-unsaturated 2-acyl pyridine, yielding β-hydroxy ketones. The tables summarize the catalytic parameters obtained for the artificial metalloenzyme.

ncAAs to coordinate copper ion

Inspired by the covalent anchoring of Cu(II)-phenanthroline and Cu(II)-2,2′-bipyridine into proteins, Drienovská et al. proposed a method to construct artificial metalloenzymes by incorporating the metal-binding ncAA, (2,2′-bipyridin-5yl)alanine (BpyAla), into a protein scaffold via the amber stop codon suppression () [Citation48,Citation92]. The bipyridine moiety in the side chain of BpyAla can coordinate with metal copper ions, forming an artificial active center. Cu(II) could catalyze a model reaction of Friedel–Crafts alkylation of 2-methylindole with α, β-unsaturated 2-acylimidazoles with 98% conversion rate, but without enantioselectivity. Additionally, the mixture of LmrR scaffold and copper ions exhibited no enantioselectivity either. However, when BpyAla was incorporated at positions 19, 89, or 93 of LmrR, the e.e. values reached 29%, 49%, and 22%, respectively, although the conversion rates were only 18%, 27%, and 36%. According to previous studies, these three sites were hot spots for incorporating ncAAs. Through protein engineering, a further constructed mutant LmrR_M89BpyAla/F93W_Cu(II) exhibited a conversion rate of 94% at 83% e.e. in the alkylation reaction of 2-methyl indole with α, β-unsaturated 2-acylimidazoles (). Other multidrug resistance regulator proteins sharing a similar hydrophobic pocket, namely QacR, CgmR, and RamR, were also utilized as scaffolds. Great performance was achieved with the QacR_Y123BpyA variant, demonstrating a substantial conversion rate of 82% and a high e.e. value of 94% [Citation93].

A comprehensive understanding of the catalytic mechanisms and spatial arrangements of catalytic residues in natural enzymes paves the way for designing artificial enzymes with functionalities [Citation94]. One challenging reaction is the hydration of ketenes, yielding β-hydroxy ketones with high atom economy. In natural enzymatic hydration, this process involves a dual catalytic mechanism, including the electrophilic activation of the ketene and the nucleophilic activation of water [Citation95,Citation96]. The artificial metalloenzyme LmrR_M89BpyAla_Cu(II) was designed to accomplish this catalytic process [Citation49]. The Lewis acidic copper ion activated the conjugated double bond through coordination with the carbonyl oxygen, while the alkaline amino acid introduced in the scaffold activated the substrate water. In order to identify an optimal site for placement of the alkaline amino acid to synergistically catalyze with the copper ion, extensive computational methods were employed. DFT calculations indicated that the distance between the side-chain carboxylate oxygen in the metal’s second coordination sphere and the substrate’s β-carbon should be maintained at 3.5 to 5 Å. Position 89 was selected for BpyAla incorporation due to its distal location within the hydrophobic pocket, thereby mitigating the risk of poorly active 2:1 ligand-to-metal complex formation. A three-dimensional structure of LmrR_M89BpyAla complexed with the α, β-unsaturated 2-acyl pyridine substrate was generated, where the substrate formed a bidentate ligand with the copper ion. GOLD and ChemScore programs were used for molecular docking and evaluation of the effect of mutations. Finally, 100 ns explicit-solvent MD simulations of the best docking model were performed to assess the conformational dynamics of the pre-designed enzyme. These in silico results predicted that LmrR_M89BpyAla would exhibit modest catalytic activity, which was confirmed experimentally, with LmrR_M89BpyA_Cu(II) achieving a 39% conversion rate and 42% enantioselectivity. The mutants, including D100E, V15E, and V15Q, closed to the ncAA-copper ion were then obtained that exhibited good conversion rates and enantioselectivity. The best mutant LmrR_M89BpyA_V15E_Cu(II) showed a conversion rate of 75% with an enantioselectivity of 64% (), significantly higher than LmrR_M89BpyA (no Cu(II)), which exhibited only a 9% conversion rate without enantioselectivity, and the single-metal catalysis reaction having a conversion rate of 83% without any selectivity [Citation49].

The synergistic catalytic effect of copper ion and the alkaline amino acid to activate two substrates offers a valuable guideline for tailoring artificial catalysts for intricate reactions. The subsequent protein engineering to modify these artificial enzymes is anticipated to further improve the conversion rate and enantioselectivity.

ncAAs to coordinate heme iron ion

The primary coordination sphere is important in controlling the catalytic properties of the metal center, and the fine control of the interaction between transition metals and ligands in artificial metalloenzymes is conducive to efficient catalysis [Citation97,Citation98]. There has been great interest in heme enzyme-mediated syntheses of useful transformations, including the insertion of non-biological carbon bases into X-H bonds and cyclopropanation reactions [Citation99,Citation100]. Natural amino acids such as cysteine, serine, aspartic acid, and histidine are often the proximal ligands of heme [Citation101,Citation102]. Recently, ncAAs like pAF and Nδ-methyl histidine (NMH) have been applied to modify the interactions between ligands and heme to improve the catalytic reaction efficiency.

Hilvert et al. incorporated NMH to replace His163 as an axial ligand into an engineered ascorbate peroxidase (APX2), finely regulating the electronic properties of the active iron-based intermediate and achieving a considerably higher turnover rate [Citation50]. The electron-donating methyl substituent on the non-coordinating Nδ atom stabilized the radical by hyperconjugation, favoring its delocalization onto the proximal NMH residue. Although the introduction of the proximal NMH ligand disrupted the catalytic Asp-His-Fe triad characteristics of the heme peroxidase, the biocatalyst still exhibited high catalytic efficiency. The turnover rate of APX2_NMH for guaiacol oxidation reaction was 31300, which was five times higher than that of APX2 (). They also employed NMH as a heme axial ligand in the myoglobin (H64V and V68A) scaffold protein. Fine-tuning of the reduction properties of iron porphyrin enabled the cyclization of styrene to cyclopropane under aerobic conditions without the need for a reducing agent [Citation103]. Compared to histidine as an axial ligand, the constructed Mb*(NMH) exhibited a doubled reduction potential (30.0 ± 3.0 mV/77.0 ± 6.0 mV), a two-fold faster reduction rate, and formed a Fe-C-N bridging structure with the iron porphyrin instead of an end-on iron-carbene.

Figure 6. ncAAs to modify heme enzymes. a. Designed BH32 protein containing NMH (PDB: 6Q7O) catalyzing the hydrolysis of fluorescein 2-phenylacetate. The tables summarize the catalytic parameters obtained for the artificial enzyme. Reprinted from graphical abstract of Ref. [Citation100] with permission. Copyright 2016 American Chemical Society. b., Designed myoglobin protein containing NMH catalyzing the cyclopropanation of styrene and ethyl diazoacetate. The tables summarize the catalytic parameters obtained for the initial non-optimized variant and the best variant after a mutagenesis study. Reprinted from graphical abstract of Ref. [Citation102] with permission. Copyright 2019 American Chemical Society. c. Styrene cyclopropanation catalyzed by Mb*-δVin-H under aerobic conditions. UV–Vis spectra showing the reduction peak of Mb*-δVin-H was significantly greater than that of Mb*-WT. Copyright 2024 Springer Nature.

Carminati et al. also constructed an artificial myoglobin (Mb_NMH) by replacing the natural heme with iron-2,4-diacetyl deuteroporphyrin IX (Fe(DADP)) and substituting the conserved axial distal histidine residue with NMH [Citation51]. The resulting artificial enzyme exhibited a significantly increased Fe3+/Fe2+ reduction potential, which was three times higher than that of the wild-type (146 ± 3 mV vs 47 ± 1 mV). This designed enzyme was able to catalyze the cyclopropanation of styrene and ethyl diazoacetate with high efficiency. Under anaerobic conditions and in the absence of any reducing agents, the reaction achieved over 99% conversion in 16 h with both enantiomeric excess and diastereomeric excess exceeding 99%, and a turnover number greater than 1000 (). Moreover, this artificial enzyme demonstrated a broad substrate scope, exhibiting high catalytic activity and excellent diastereo- and enantio-selectivity toward a variety of electron-deficient alkenes and ketones that were poorly reactive or unreactive with the wild-type heme-containing enzyme.

The Nδ-vinylhistidine (δVin-H) was also applied to evolve myoglobin by replacing His93 by δVin-H to modify the interactions between ligand and heme () [Citation45]. The yield of Mb*-δVin-H-catalyzed cyclopropanation of styrene reached 80% under the aerobic conditions of 0.1% mol of the enzyme, while Mb*-WT yielded only 30% under the same conditions. The performance of the Mb enzyme activity was greatly improved by replacing the axial ligand of the heme from His to δVin-H under aerobic conditions. Similar improvements in enzyme activity were also observed for the electron-rich styrene substrate. The above results revealed that integrating structurally diverse ncAAs into metalloenzymes through expanded genetic codes has the potential to greatly expand the range of available coordination environments, and hence enhanced the catalytic efficiency of diverse artificial enzymes.

Design of artificial photoenzymes with ncAAs

Photosensitive biocatalysts harness the precise selectivity of enzymes and the distinct catalytic pathways of photocatalysts to accomplish various significant bond syntheses, such as C − H bond activation, C − C bond cross-coupling, C − N bond formation, and halogenation [Citation104,Citation105]. Natural photocatalytic enzymes employ photoactive cofactors like flavin or nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (phosphate) (NAD(P)H). However, only a few enzymes, such as protochlorophyllide reductase, photolyase for DNA repair, and fatty acid decarboxylase, possess this capability [Citation106–108]. Constructing artificial photoenzymes may involve covalently attaching photocatalysts with ncAAs to facilitate the catalytic process via a single-electron transfer mechanism. Alternatively, the ncAAs as photosensitizers can be directly integrated into a designed protein scaffold to enable triplet-state energy transfer for catalysis.

Benzophenone-alanine (BpA) is an ncAA featuring a benzophenone side chain, a highly effective organic photosensitizer. Upon irradiation with light at specific wavelengths, benzophenone efficiently undergoes intersystem crossing to a long-lived triplet state (τ = 50 μs) with nearly 100% efficiency, possessing a high triplet energy (ET = 69.1 kcal mol−1) [Citation109]. Liu et al. utilized BpA coupling with the transition metal Ni to construct a genetically encoded 27 kDa photosensitive protein (PSP), which successfully achieved CO2 reduction with a quantum efficiency of 2.6% for CO2/CO conversion [Citation52]. In the study, the chromophore Tyr66 of the superfolder yellow fluorescent protein was substituted with BpA, which exhibited a longer excited state lifetime and stronger reducing capability (E0 = −1.49 V in DMF). In the wild-type protein, Tyr66 participated in the autocatalytic conversion of the highly fluorescent hydroxybenzylidene-5-imidazolinone moiety through the tripeptide Gly65-Tyr66-Gly67. It was proposed that Gly65-BpA66-Gly67 could undergo a similar conversion, leading to a chromophore with a significantly prolonged triplet excited state lifetime. Upon interaction with a sacrificial reductant, this chromophore transitioned into a highly reduced radical with an exceptionally low redox potential of −1.5 V. By combining this engineered fluorescent protein with an organic Ni-terpyridine cofactor, the photoreduction of CO2 with an overall turnover number exceeding 100 and a quantum yield of 2.6% was achieved (). This performance surpassed those most well-known photocatalysts for CO2 reduction. The performance of the catalyst could be optimized by adjusting the distance between the catalyst/chromophore and its microenvironment designed through site-directed TAG mutagenesis.

Figure 7. Design of artificial photoenzymes with BpA. a. Incorporation of BpA into the superfolder yellow fluorescent protein (PDB: 3ED8) to construct photoenzymes. The process of the tripeptide Gly65-BpA66-Gly67 converted into a chromophore that contains (E)−4-(4-benzoylbenzylidene)-1,2-dimethyl-1H-imidazole-5(4H)-one (Bp-imidizolinone) and the diagram of metal chelation involved in the construction of artificial photoenzymes.

In another study, a genetically engineered photosensitizer-based artificial dehalogenase was designed for the biocatalytic cross-coupling of aryl halides. The bipyridine (bpy) was first covalently coupled to Cys95 of PSP, which was subsequently complexed with Ni, generating an artificial metalloenzyme PSP-95C-NiII(bpy) () [Citation53]. Using the cross-coupling of p-bromobenzaldehyde with water as a model reaction, the designed photosensitizer metalloenzyme achieved a conversion yield of 98% for p-bromobenzaldehyde. Furthermore, this artificial metalloenzyme exhibited the capability to form C-N bonds, achieving coupling yields of 39% and 57% for imidazole and dihydropyrrole, respectively. It was observed from the fluorescence quenching experiments that the substrate molecule p-bromobenzaldehyde could influence the lifetime of PSP even in the absence of NiII(bpy) in the system [Citation54]. This observation suggested that the PSP protein possessed an exceptionally long-lived radical state with potent reducing capabilities, potentially enabling the direct reductive dehalogenation of aryl halides. When using sodium formate (HCOONa) as a sacrificial reducing agent, the direct reductive dehalogenation of p-bromobenzaldehyde to benzaldehyde was achieved at a high yield of 99% () by PSP. Similarly, remarkable conversions were also achieved for iodinated and chlorinated substrates [Citation54].

Wu et al. first docked BpA into the interfacial pocket of LmrR (PDB: 3F8F) and preferentially selected residues V15, N19, M89, and F93 based on their spatial proximity (< 10 Å) to the C(2)-C(3) double bond of substrate [Citation55]. The best engineered photoenzyme LmrR_F93BpA (TPe1.0) successfully catalyzed the cyclization of indole derivatives, achieving a 51% yield without enantioselectivity. Through three rounds of directed evolution, LmrRF93BpA/W96L/M8L demonstrated 58% yield and with 71% e.e. in the model cyclization reaction. Although benzophenone alone catalyzed the model reaction with a yield of 89%, it lacked enantioselectivity. The ongoing evolution of the scaffold protein has significantly enhanced the performance of the engineered artificial enzyme (). Green et al. successfully engineered the artificial photolyase EnT1.0 (BpA) by genetically incorporating BpA into the scaffold protein DA_20_00, which enabled the intramolecular [2 + 2] cycloaddition of quinolone via triplet-state energy transfer of BpA with 9% conversion rate and 46% e.e. () [Citation56]. Through multiple rounds of evolution, the optimized enzyme EnT1.0(BpA)/M90A/Q149D/P196R/K225E/A229S exhibited a remarkably 10-fold enhancement in reaction conversion compared to EnT1.0(BpA) after 30 min of irradiation.

Figure 8. Design of artificial photoenzymes with BpA and thio. a. Illustration of artificial photoenzymes. LmrR_F93BpA (PDB: 7XUP) catalyzing the cyclization of indole derivatives, and EnT1.0(BpA) (PDB: 7ZP5) facilitating the intramolecular [2 + 2] cycloaddition of quinolone. b. Development of artificial photoenzymes using iodoacetamide-cysteine bioconjugation in the scaffold protein LmrR to catalyze the cycloaddition of 2-carboxamide indoles. The tables summarize the catalytic parameters obtained for the initial non-optimized variant and the best variant after a mutagenesis study. Copyright 2024 American Chemical Society.

Chemical modification is another effective approach for incorporating ncAAs to construct artificial enzymes. Wu et al. employed iodoacetamide-cysteine bio-conjugation in the scaffold protein LmrR to develop an artificial photoenzyme, creating an enzyme library containing benzophenone, oxazolone, thioxanthenone (thio), fluorenone, and anthraquinone () [Citation57]. The enzyme variant LmrR_thio_93C demonstrated the best performance for the cycloaddition of 2-carboxamide indoles, achieving 88% yield and 53:47 e.r. After four rounds of iterative mutations targeting amino acids around the active site, the optimized variant LmrR_thio_93C/W96L/M8L/N88H was obtained, exhibiting superior activity and selectivity (95% yield, 96:4 e.r.). The incorporation of ncAAs has long been a challenging aspect of enzyme design, often requiring extensive engineering of aminoacyl-tRNA synthetases with a low success rate. This example presents an alternative approach, showcasing the feasibility of incorporating key catalytic groups into proteins via chemical conjugation, providing a new perspective on enzyme design. However, protein scaffolds often contain more than one cysteine, making targeted chemical modification challenging. Mutating excess cysteine residues can destabilize the protein, as they often stabilize its structure by forming disulfide bonds.

Integrating chemical catalysts with artificial photoenzymes development creates novel hybrid catalysts, vastly expanding the diversity and innovative potential of biocatalysts. This strategy not only broadens the avenues for creating eco-friendly biocatalysts but also imparts enzymes with entirely new chemical reactivity by incorporating ncAAs and photosensitizers. Nonetheless, the construction of artificial photoenzymes encounters various hurdles, including the design of reactions and the incorporation of catalytic components. We eagerly anticipate the unveiling of additional successful instances that showcase advancements and accomplishments in this field [Citation97,Citation110].

Artificial intelligence for de novo design enzyme with ncAAs

The Nobel Prize in Chemistry 2024 was awarded to David Baker “for computational protein design”, and to Demis Hassabis and John M. Jumper “for protein structure prediction”. Deep learning has demonstrated remarkable atomic-scale precision in predicting protein structures, highlighting its significant potential for enzyme design [Citation111,Citation112]. De novo enzyme design has evolved from rudimentary, physics‐based methods to sophisticated, data‐driven platforms capable of crafting entirely novel protein sequences and folds—moving beyond simple modifications of natural enzymes to achieve desired functionalities [Citation113,Citation114]. Tools including AlphaFold and RoseTTAFold can predict protein structures and interactions with near-experimental accuracy [Citation115,Citation116]. Furthermore, models such as Chroma and RFdiffusion specifically address the motif-to-scaffold challenge in protein design [Citation14,Citation113]. By fine-tuning RoseTTAFold’s all-atom diffusion framework, RFdiffusion-AA enables protein backbone folding around small-molecule ligands, greatly simplifying the design of proteins for binding specific targets [Citation117]. Deep-learning-based models, including ProteinMPNN and LigandMPNN, remodel protein side chains, demonstrating excellent performance in generating proteins with enhanced solubility, thermostability, and even activity [Citation15,Citation118–121].

Several artificial photoenzymes have been designed by the installation of genetically encoded photosensitizer into the de novo designed protein scaffold [Citation122,Citation123]. The engineering of artificial photoenzymes requires precise synergistic control of protein scaffolds and photosensitive moieties due to their inherently complex catalytic mechanisms. It is anticipated that the Family‑wide Hallucination algorithm can sample native photoenzyme scaffolds to generate more functionally rational artificial protein scaffolds [Citation124]. OA-ReactDiff, based on the diffusion model framework rapidly and accurately used to predict transition-state geometries between reactants and products, enabling the precise design of photoenzymes’ active sites [Citation125]. Protein language models like ESM enable zero‑shot guidance of protein evolution, and their strategic integration with automated biofoundries could accelerate the engineering of artificial photoenzymes [Citation126].

Recent advances have successfully recapitulated native enzyme catalytic functions—including lysozymes, luciferases, recombinases, proteases, phenylalanine hydroxylases, haloalkane dehalogenases, chorismate mutases, malate dehydrogenases, carbonic anhydrases, and lactate dehydrogenases—marking key milestones in AI-driven enzyme engineering [Citation127–133]. Despite these advances, the key challenge remains the precise engineering of artificial enzymes with specific catalytic activity. Additionally, all current deep learning-based enzyme design tools are confined to generating proteins solely with natural amino acids, posing difficulties in the design of active centers incorporating ncAAs. Also, traditional computational design methods based on physical models are also powerful in the development of new biocatalysts, with notable achievements in designing enzymes such as carboxylic acid reductase, serine hydrolases, and porphyrin-containing proteins [Citation134–136]. However, currently, the process of designing enzymes containing ncAAs often depends on a semi-rational approach informed by prior experience and the known catalytic properties of ncAAs. Well-known molecular modeling software such as Rosetta currently supports parameters for only a few ncAAs like BpyAla, highlighting a considerable need for the expansion of parameters to cover a broader spectrum of ncAAs [Citation137]. The increasing number of enzymes containing ncAAs would aid in resolving design challenges with deep-learning tools. It is anticipated that future advancements in computational tools, including both deep learning models and physical models, would enable the direct creation of selective and efficient catalytic sites tailored to specific reactions, with the thorough consideration of the ncAA structure and the dynamics of the reaction intermediates.

Outlook

Chemists have developed many effective small-molecule and transition metal catalysts. The combination of the catalytic properties of these catalysts with the spatial and regional selectivity of proteins is gaining attraction among enzyme designers. Design of artificial enzymes with novel functions by incorporation of ncAAs possessing various non-biological catalytic groups is a vital approach to broaden the range of biocatalysts, allowing them to catalyze chemical reactions that natural enzymes are unable to perform. The artificial enzymes containing ncAAs also offer a starting point for directed evolution, no matter whether ncAAs act directly as a catalytic group or as a biological component that chelates metals. This represents a promising strategy to obtain enzymes with novel functionalities.

Artificial enzymes, such as the catalytically active nanomaterials, so-called nanozymes, have demonstrated broad application prospects in multiple fields such as food packaging, environmental monitoring and remediation, biosensing, biomedical diagnosis, and tumor therapy due to their excellent stability and multifunctionality () [Citation153–156]. More importantly, several such artificial enzymes have reached commercial production, such as hemin-based peroxidase mimics used in fecal occult blood test kits and artificial nitrogenase for the hydrogenation of N2 to produce the ammonia fertilizer [Citation149,Citation157]. However, compared to the nanozymes, few artificial enzymes based on protein scaffolds have been applied in industry, mainly due to their moderate activity or enantioselectivity [Citation158]. The artificial enzymes containing ncAAs also face significant challenges in commercial application due to the high cost of ncAAs synthesis and the low incorporation efficiency [Citation159].

Table 2. Applications of artificial enzymes across diverse fields and their key functional roles.

Download CSVDisplay Table

GCE technology has enabled the successful incorporation of over four hundred ncAAs into proteins, including analogs of natural amino acids, metal ligands, radicals, and so on. However, the availability of these monomers presents a challenge due to limited commercial availability and high costs [Citation160]. The customization of specific ncAA structures often requires substantial human and financial resources, posing difficulties for biological researchers lacking expertise in organic synthesis. Developing new biosynthetic pathways for ncAAs that can be integrated into host cell metabolism offers a promising solution, potentially enabling the cost-effective production of ncAAs and facilitating the design of enzymes containing these amino acids through genetic code expansion [Citation161–163]. As technical barriers like low incorporation efficiency and high cost are progressively overcome, ncAAs are anticipated to play a more important role in developing novel enzymes.

Statement of significance

This review presents a novel strategy for designing artificial enzymes by incorporating noncanonical amino acids (ncAAs) with catalytic functions into protein scaffolds through genetic code expansion. These ncAAs can serve as catalytic groups, metal-coordinating groups, heme ligands, and photocatalytic groups. By combining the unique chemical reactivity of ncAAs with the structural precision of protein scaffolds, this approach significantly broadens the chemical space of biocatalysis. The review also highlights the potential of integrating artificial intelligence to accelerate de novo enzyme design.

Disclosure statement

The authors report there are no competing interests to declare.

            (Open in a new window)Web of Science ®(Open in a new window)Google Scholar
          • Shao L, Wang X, Du X, et al. Application of multifunctional nanozymes in tumor therapy. ACS Omega. 2024;9:15753–15767. doi: 10.1021/acsomega.4c00258.
          • Ai Y, Hu Z-N, Liang X, et al. Recent advances in nanozymes: from matters to bioapplications. Adv Funct Materials. 2022;32:2110432. doi: 10.1002/adfm.202110432.
          • Liang M, Yan X. Nanozymes: from new concepts, mechanisms, and standards to applications. Acc Chem Res. 2019;52:2190–2200. doi: 10.1021/acs.accounts.9b00140.
          • Lovelock SL, Crawshaw R, Basler S, et al. The road to fully programmable protein catalysis. Nature. 2022;606:49–58. doi: 10.1038/s41586-022-04456-z.
          • Brouwer B, Della-Felice F, Illies JH, et al. Noncanonical amino acids: bringing new-to-nature functionalities to biocatalysis. Chem Rev. 2024;124:10877–10923. doi: 10.1021/acs.chemrev.4c00136.
          • Gfeller D, Michielin O, Zoete V. SwissSidechain: a molecular and structural database of non-natural sidechains. Nucleic Acids Res. 2013;41: d 327–332. doi: 10.1093/nar/gks991.
          • Almhjell PJ, Boville CE, Arnold FH. Engineering enzymes for noncanonical amino acid synthesis. Chem Soc Rev. 2018;47:8980–8997. doi: 10.1039/c8cs00665b.
          • Mehl RA, Anderson JC, Santoro SW, et al. Generation of a bacterium with a 21 amino acid genetic code. J Am Chem Soc. 2003;125:935–939. doi: 10.1021/ja0284153.
          • Wang Y, Chen X, Cai W, et al. Expanding the structural diversity of protein building blocks with noncanonical amino acids biosynthesized from aromatic thiols. Angew Chem Int Ed Engl. 2021;60:10040–10048. doi: 10.1002/anie.202014540.
